coredata.dc:title: The precursors’ feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O induced the modulation of hydrothermal reaction products and their photocatalytic properties

coredata.dc:description: Regulating the composition and proportion of heterojunctions is critical to improve their photocatalytic properties. Many bismuth-based oxides are readily converted from one oxide to another under certain conditions, which supplies a simple strategy for the in-situ construction of bismuth-based oxides heterostructures with controllable composition and proportion. Herein, a series of tunable products from NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite (NCQDs: nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots) to Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction to pure Bi2O2CO3 were obtained just by adjusting the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O precursors’ feeding ratio during the hydrothermal process, in which NCQDs act as an active component or carbon source dependant on the feeding ratio. Both NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructures exhibit enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for removing methyl orange (MO) and bisphenol A (BPA) under simulated sunlight, compared with Bi2O4 alone. Nevertheless, the latter is more effective in promoting the catalytic activity of Bi2O4 than the former. The reaction rate constant k of the optimal NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid is only 1.6 and 2.37 times higher than that of single Bi2O4, whereas the k of the optimal Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction is about 2.93 and 3.07 times higher than that of single Bi2O4 for the degradation of MO and BPA, respectively. The much higher photoinduced charges’ separation efficiency, verified by the transient photocurrent response and EIS results, should be the main reason the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction possesses superior photocatalytic activity to the NCQDs/Bi2O4 heterostructure. Hole (h+) and superoxide radical (•O2 −) are determined to be the primary and secondary important active species for the photodegradation reaction, respectively. This work supplies a convenient strategy to modulate the composition and proportion of the hydrothermal reaction products and their photocatalytic properties through changing the precursors’ feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Regulating the composition and proportion of heterojunctions is critical to improve their photocatalytic properties. Many bismuth-based oxides are readily converted from one oxide to another under certain conditions, which supplies a simple strategy for the in-situ construction of bismuth-based oxides heterostructures with controllable composition and proportion. Herein, a series of tunable products from NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite (NCQDs: nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots) to Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction to pure Bi2O2CO3 were obtained just by adjusting the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O precursors’ feeding ratio during the hydrothermal process, in which NCQDs act as an active component or carbon source dependant on the feeding ratio. Both NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructures exhibit enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for removing methyl orange (MO) and bisphenol A (BPA) under simulated sunlight, compared with Bi2O4 alone. Nevertheless, the latter is more effective in promoting the catalytic activity of Bi2O4 than the former. The reaction rate constant k of the optimal NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid is only 1.6 and 2.37 times higher than that of single Bi2O4, whereas the k of the optimal Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction is about 2.93 and 3.07 times higher than that of single Bi2O4 for the degradation of MO and BPA, respectively. The much higher photoinduced charges’ separation efficiency, verified by the transient photocurrent response and EIS results, should be the main reason the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction possesses superior photocatalytic activity to the NCQDs/Bi2O4 heterostructure. Hole (h+) and superoxide radical (•O2 −) are determined to be the primary and secondary important active species for the photodegradation reaction, respectively. This work supplies a convenient strategy to modulate the composition and proportion of the hydrothermal reaction products and their photocatalytic properties through changing the precursors’ feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a family, bismuth-based oxides are fascinating photocatalytic materials [1]. Their optical response ranges span the UV, visible, and near-infrared regions [2]. Up to date, many bismuth-based oxides, like BiOX (x=Cl, Br, I) [3,4], Bi2O2CO3 [5], Bi2O3 [6], BiVO4 [7], Bi2WO6 [8–11], Bi2O4 and BiO2-x [12], have been investigated widely in the field of photocatalysis, including environmental renovation and green energy production. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of single bismuth-based oxide is far from satisfactory due to its intrinsic drawbacks, such as limited optical absorption capability and fast recombination of photogenerated carriers. The combination of two or more photoactive materials to construct heterojunction is widely adopted to extend the spectral response range and enhance the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [13].

One prominent feature of bismuth-based oxides is their conversion from one oxide to another under mild conditions, which is very helpful for the in-situ creation of bismuth-based oxide junctions with intensified interfacial interaction. For instance, Zhou et al. revealed the synthesis of Bi2O2CO3 from Bi2O3 through stirring the aqueous suspension of Bi2O3 at room temperature, using the atmospheric CO2 as a carbon source [14]. In turn, Bi2O2CO3 could also transfer to Bi2O3 once the calcination temperature is over 350 °C [15,16]. This mutual conversion between Bi2O3 and Bi2O2CO3 contributes to the in-situ construction of Bi2O3/Bi2O2CO3 hybrid. By transforming BiOCl into Bi2O3 in an alkaline environment, Bi2O3/BiOCl heterojunction was facilely obtained using BiOCl as a self-sacrificed template [17]. Based on the solubility difference between Bi2O3 and BiVO4, the growth of BiVO4 on the Bi2O3 sacrificial surface was developed for the Bi2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction formation recently [18].

Apart from the above-mentioned transformation of Bi(III) oxides, NaBiO3·2H2O, as a typical Bi(V) oxide, not only could experience the change from NaBiO3·2H2O to other bismuth oxides but also accompanied with the variation of bismuth chemical valence under certain conditions, resulting in the formation of variable bismuth oxides [19]. Bi2O4, a mixed-valence Bi(III&V) oxide visible light photocatalyst, has attracted considerable attention recently [19,20]. It is usually synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment using NaBiO3·2H2O as a bismuth source and deionized (DI) water as a medium [21], which is a representative conversion from NaBiO3·2H2O to Bi2O4. Through adjusting the hydrothermal conditions, such as the volume of water, the dosage of NaOH and NaBiO3·2H2O and the hydrothermal reaction temperature, a series of Bi2O4/BiO2-x, Bi4O7/BiO2-x, and Bi2O4/Bi4O7/BiO2-x composite have been successfully prepared, which provides an efficient strategy for the construction of mixed-valence Bi(III&V) oxides junction [12,22,23]. Supposing carbon source like melem, the building unit of g-C3N4, and Na2CO3 is introduced into the hydrothermal system, Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructure can be easily produced because the carbon source readily reacts with NaBiO3·2H2O to generate Bi2O2CO3 and then the residual NaBiO3·2H2O is transformed into Bi2O4 during the hydrothermal process [24,25].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of carbonaceous nanomaterials, have shown widespread applications because of their superb water solubility, chemical stability, low cost, environmentally friendliness, up-conversion fluorescence property and electron mediator feature [26–29]. Given the superior optical and electron transfer properties, CQDs are often used to modify the semiconductors to enhance their catalytic efficiency [30]. In our recent work, NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid was developed to improve the photodegradation efficiency of Bi2O4 under a broad spectrum range [31]. Similarly, Long et al. [32] also reported that NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite exhibited higher visible light photodegradation performance for the removal of MO and phenol than Bi2O4 alone [32]. However, the above two works only utilized a very low NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio, less than 1.51%, to prepare the NCQDs/Bi2O4 heterostructure by hydrothermal method. If raising the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio during the hydrothermal process, what would be the final products? In responding to this question, a much larger NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio range (1.5∼12%) was selected to experience the typical hydrothermal reaction used to synthesize Bi2O4. Interestingly, besides the NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid, the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composite can also be obtained, which depends on the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio. Even the pure Bi2O2CO3 can be produced as long as the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio is high enough, such as 12%. These phenomena reflect that a low dosage of NCQDs, as one active component, combines with the in-situ formed Bi2O4, leading to the formation of the NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid. However, a relatively high dosage of NCQDs, as a carbon source, reacts with partial NaBiO3·2H2O to generate Bi2O2CO3, resulting in the generation of Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composite. Therefore, adjusting the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio can realize the regulation of hydrothermal reaction products from NCQDs/Bi2O4 to Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 to Bi2O2CO3, which has never been reported previously.

Herein, NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions were prepared by an in-situ one pot hydrothermal method, through adjusting the precursors’ feeding ratio of NCQDs to NaBiO3·2H2O. The catalytic activities of NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions were estimated by the catalytic removal of MO and BPA under simulated solar light irradiation. Although both NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructures exhibit superior photocatalytic performance to Bi2O4 alone, the latter is more effective in promoting the catalytic activity of Bi2O4 than the former. h+ and •O2 − are the primary and secondary important active species for the photodegradation reaction, respectively. A probable photodegradation mechanism is put forward for the NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junction, respectively.

The commercial NaBiO3·2H2O and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. C6H8O7·H2O, H2NCONH2, Na2SO4 and isopropanol (IPA) were supplied by Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Bi2O4 and NCQDs were prepared according to the previous reported methods [21,32]. NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions were synthesized by an in situ one-pot hydrothermal reactions. Typically, a certain content of NaBiO3·2H2O was dispersed in a certain volume of NCQDs solution, and then deionized (DI) water was added to keep the total volume of the mixture at 60 mL. The above solution was filled into a 100 mL sealed autoclave. After stirring for 20 min, the autoclave was kept at 160°C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained samples were washed with DI water and ethanol. The catalysts were denoted as NCQDs/BO-X or BO/BOC-X, where X signifies the mass ratio of NCQDs/ NaBiO3·2H2O, and BO & BOC represent the Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3, respectively. If X≤2, the obtained samples were marked as NCQDs/BO-X. When 2<X≤11, the gained products were named BO/BOC-X. Once X was up to 12, the final product was observed as pure Bi2O2CO3.

X-ray diffraction (Japan, XRD6100) was used to investigate the crystalline structure of photocatalysts. The infrared absorption spectra of the samples were recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20). FESEM (Zeiss, Sigma) and TEM (FEIG2) were used to observe the morphology and microstructure of catalysts. UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to obtain the UV-visible absorbance spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, KRATOS Analytical AXISHSi spectrometer) was used to estimate the samples’ composition and chemical status. The automated gas sorption analyzer (Quantachrome ASIQM000-200-6) was used to acquire N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and specific surface areas. Bruker A200 spectrometer (Germany) was used to record the electron spin resonance (ESR) data. Electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E) was used to measure the transient photocurrent response and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

MO (15 mg/L) and bisphenol A (BPA, 20 mg/L) were degraded to access the photodegradation efficiency of catalysts under simulated sunlight irradiation. Typically, 24 mg catalysts were introduced into 58 mL MO or BPA solution. Then, the solution was vigorously stirred in the dark for 60 min to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that, the solution was irradiated with simulated sunlight. At certain time intervals, 4 mL solution was collected and filtered to remove the photocatalysts and analyzed with a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The trapping experiments were performed by adding isopropanol (IPA), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), p-benzoquinone (BQ), or purging Ar2 to the system to detect the active species during the photocatalytic degradation process. Other experiment procedures were similar to the above photodegradation experiment. In the cycle experiments, the samples collected by centrifugationand washed with deionized water and ethanol were redispersed into fresh contaminant solution for next cycle.

All samples were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method, and the preparation procedure is exhibited in Scheme 1 . As can be seen from Scheme 1, the final products of the hydrothermal reaction were strongly dependent on the precursors’ feeding ratio, namely the mass ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O, which is represented by X. The crystalline structure of catalysts was measured by the XRD technique. As described in Fig. 1 a, pristine Bi2O4 could be facilely synthesized by the typical hydrothermal method using the sole NaBiO3·2H2O precursor since all the peaks are well matched with the crystal planes of the monoclinic phase Bi2O4 (JCPDS 50–0864) [33]. When a small amount of NCQDs is added, like X=1.5 and 2, the characteristic peaks of NCQDs/BO-1.5 and NCQDs/BO-2 samples are the same as those of pure Bi2O4. This means the formation of NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid because a trace amount of NCQDs is difficult to be detected by the XRD technique. Similar results were also reported by recent works [31,32]. Once the feeding ratio X increases to 3 for the BO/BOC-3 sample, apart from the peaks of Bi2O4, new diffraction peaks of Bi2O2CO3 begin to appear, implying the initial generation of the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composite. Further raising the X to 4, the peak intensities of Bi2O2CO3 are even stronger than those of Bi2O4, suggesting the increased content of Bi2O2CO3 in the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 hybrid. If the X gradually ascends in the range of 5-11, this trend is further strengthened. Namely, the more NCQDs added, the higher percentage of Bi2O2CO3 in the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junction. Even the pure Bi2O2CO3 can be produced if only sufficient NCQDs is introduced into the reaction system, such as X=12. Its characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ= 12.9, 23.9, 26.0, 30.3, and 39.5° are consistent with (002), (011), (004), (013), and (006) crystal planes of Bi2O2CO3 (JCPDS-41-1448) [34,35], respectively, showing that the crystal phase structure is tetragonal Bi2O2CO3. The above XRD results demonstrate that the final products, including the components and the proportion between constituents, can be easily regulated by altering the feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O during the hydrothermal reaction. NCQDs precursor doesn't take part in the reaction but only serves as one component to combine with Bi2O4, derived from the NaBiO3·2H2O during the hydrothermal process, forming the NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite if the precursors’ feeding ratio is no more than 2% [31,32]. Once the feeding ratio X is up to 3%, NCQDs precursor, as the carbon source, directly participates in the reaction to generate Bi2O2CO3, and then the residual NaBiO3·2H2O transfers to Bi2O4 during the hydrothermal reaction, producing the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 product. Furthermore, the Bi2O2CO3 content in the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 hybrid gradually rises with the increase of feeding ratio X [24,25,36]. It is expected that NaBiO3·2H2O would be completely converted to Bi2O2CO3, leading to the generation of pure Bi2O2CO3 as long as the feeding ratio X is high enough. Thus, the regulation of the hydrothermal reaction products from NCQDs/Bi2O4 to Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 to pure Bi2O2CO3 can be facilely achieved through changing the two precursors’ feeding ratio of NCQDs to NaBiO3·2H2O.

To further confirm the above-mentioned trend, FT-IR spectra measurement was performed on Bi2O4, NCQDs/BO-1.5, BO/BOC-7 and Bi2O2CO3 four samples, which are produced under the X at 0, 1.5, 7 and 12, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1b, in the spectrum of pure Bi2O4, the peak at 551 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the Bi-O bond in Bi2O4 [37,38]. For pure Bi2O2CO3, the band at 845 cm−1 should correspond to the out-of-plane bending mode of CO3 2− and the strong peak at 1388 cm−1 can be assigned to the antisymmetric vibrational mode of CO3 2− [39]. When the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio is relatiely low, as shown in the spectra of NCQDs/BO-1.5, besides the characteristic peak of Bi2O4, the absorption bands of NCQDs are also observed at 3342 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 respectively[40]. The 3342 cm−1 broad absorption band is the typical stretching vibration of O-H, corresponding to the -OH functional group on the surface of the carbon quantum dots and/or adsorbed water molecules, while the other absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 can be ascribed to the C=O stretching vibration, which is consistent with the previous FTIR results of NCQDs [40]. When the mass ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O is further increased, such as BO/BOC-7 sample, its spectra contain the characteristic absorption bands of both Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4, confirming the co-existence of Bi2O3CO3 and Bi2O4 components in the hybrid. Therefore, the FTIR results support again the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio on the modulation of the hydrothermal reaction product.

The morphology and microstructure of Bi2O4, Bi2O2CO3, NCQDs/BO-X, and BO/BOC-X heterojunctions were characterized by SEM and TEM techniques. Fig. 2 a shows a typical SEM image of Bi2O4, in which many irregular submicronrods with a length in the micron level can be easily observed. Pure Bi2O2CO3, synthesized at a high feeing ratio, such as X=12, looks like smooth thick flakes with a rectangular or square shape (Fig. 2b). From the TEM image of NCQDs in Fig. S1, it can be seen that the NCQDs show a uniform dot shape with good dispersion and their size is about less than 5 nm. For the NCQDs/BO-1.5 and NCQDs/BO-2 composites, since the size of NCQDs dispersed on the Bi2O4 surface is too small to be observed by SEM, they also exhibit a submicron rod-like morphology, similar to the appearance of pure Bi2O4 (Fig, 2c, d), but the EDS mapping (Fig. S2) shows that Bi, O, C and N elements are uniformly dispersed in NCQDs/BO-1.5, which means the formation of NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid. This result indicates that a relatively low feeing ratio X contributes to generating NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite. As for BO/BOC-3, besides the rod-liked Bi2O4, it is difficult to find the existence of flake-shaped Bi2O2CO3 (Fig. 2e). The reason may be the growth of Bi2O2CO3 in its infancy owing to the relatively low dosage of NCQDs. Thus, the size of Bi2O2CO3 in the BO/BOC-3 composite is not large enough to be observed through SEM. When X reaches to 4, namely BO/BOC-4 sample, the irregular plate products corresponding to Bi2O2CO3, can be detected, and the plate-liked Bi2O2CO3 interacts with the rod-liked Bi2O4, forming a heterostructured interface between Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4 (Fig. 2f), consistent with the result of XRD. With the increase of X, this phenomenon becomes increasingly apparent, as can be seen from Figs. 2g to 2j. For instance, it is clear that many planar Bi2O2CO3 products can be easily found, and Bi2O4 rods are in close contact with Bi2O2CO3 plates, producing numerous Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions in the BO/BOC-5 and BO/BOC-7 samples (Figs. 2g, h). Further raising the X to 9∼11, the content of Bi2O2CO3 flakes in the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructures is also further increased. Furthermore, the size of plane Bi2O2CO3 becomes larger and larger, while that of rod Bi2O4 becomes smaller and smaller (Figs. 2i, j), implying that more NaBiO3·2H2O is transformed into Bi2O2CO3. Clearly, the results of SEM images agree well with the results of XRD. Once again, these results directly prove that the low dosage of NCQDs doesn't take part in the reaction to form Bi2O2CO3 but combines with Bi2O4 evolved from the NaBiO3·2H2O during the hydrothermal process to produce the NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid. However, the high dosage of NCQDs, as the carbon source, reacts with NaBiO3·2H2O to generate Bi2O2CO3, and the residual NaBiO3·2H2O will be converted to Bi2O4 during the hydrothermal process, resulting in the generation of Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction. Furthermore, the content of Bi2O2CO3 in the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructure can be easily tuned by regulating the NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeing ratio during the hydrothermal reaction. TEM measurement was further implemented to observe the generation of the heterostructure. Taking the BO/BOC-7 as an example, it is obvious that the rod-like Bi2O4 particles are attached onto the sheet-like Bi2O2CO3 to form Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions, which is in accordance with the SEM results. These heterostructures with a large close contact area provide a good platform for the transport of carriers, which is propitious to the separation of photoinduced carriers, thereby enhancing the catalytic performance [41].

For the purpose of investigating the elemental composition and chemical state of samples, Bi2O4, NCQDs/BO-1.5, and BO/BOC-7 were selected to carry out the XPS characterization. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, Bi, O, and C elements are found in Bi2O4, NCQDs/BO-1.5, and BO/BOC-7. The unexpected C1s signal in Bi2O4 might come from the carbon-based contaminants in the vacuum system [42]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum in the NCQDs/Bi2O4 can be fitted to three peaks at 288.7 eV, 286.2eV, and 284.8 eV, which can belong to the C=O bond, C–N bond, and adventitious carbon species from XPS measurement (Fig. 3b), respectively [43]. These results strongly demonstrate that NCQDs have been successfully anchored onto the Bi2O4 surface, forming the NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid when a low NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeing ratio is adopted during the hydrothermal reaction. For the C 1s peak of BO/BOC-7, the one peak at 284.8eV is assigned to adventitious carbon contaminant and the other two peaks at 286.3eV and 288.6eV are observed, which indicates the presence of O-bearing bonding (C–OH) [44] and the carbonate ion in Bi2O2CO3, respectively[45]. Clearly, the XPS results testify NCQDs again as carbon resource, react with NaBiO3·2H2O to produce Bi2O2CO3 if a relatively high NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O feeding ratio is applied during the hydrothermal process, which is helpful for the in-situ construction of Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction. Therefore, it can be rationally concluded from the above XRD, SEM, TEM, and XPS results that the final constituent of products in the hydrothermal reaction can be adjusted by modulating the precursor feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O.

As described in Fig. 3c, the high-resolution Bi 4f spectra of Bi2O4, NCQDs/BO-1.5, and BO/BOC-7 can be fitted to two bimodal peaks. For pristine Bi2O4, the peaks of Bi 4f7/2 are centered at 158.3 and 159.0 eV, and the peaks of Bi 4f5/2 are situated at 163.6 and 164.2 eV, respectively, identifying the presence of Bi(III) and Bi(V) in Bi2O4 [20,46]. For NCQDs/BO-1.5, its peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 are centered at 158.5eV (159.2eV) and 163.9 eV (164.5 eV), respectively. Compared with single Bi2O4, the Bi 4f binding energies of NCQDs/Bi2O4-1.5 have an obvious positive shift, indicating the occurrence of electrons transfer from Bi2O4 to NCQDs in the NCQDs/BO-1.5 hybrid [42]. Similarly, in contrast to pristine Bi2O4, the binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 for BO/BOC-7 also exhibit a slight shift to higher energy, implying the presence of interfacial charge interaction between Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3, which can encourage the migration and separation of interfacial charges. In the spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 3d), the O 1s peaks in Bi2O4 can be deconvoluted to three peaks corresponding to lattice oxygen (528.9 eV) and chemisorbed oxygen (529.7 eV) and physisorbed oxygen (530.8 eV), respectively [47,48]. Significantly, the binding energy of lattice O in NCQDs/BO-1.5 transfers towards higher binding energy, which can further suggest a strong interaction between Bi2O4 and NCQDs. Similarly, for BO/BOC-7, the binding energies of O 1s are transferred to 529.3 eV, 530.1 eV, and 531.3 eV, respectively. The apparent shift towards relatively higher binding energy of Bi4f and O1s of lattice oxygen in BO/BOC-7 heterojunction can be attributed to the strong interfacial interaction between Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3, which can promote the interfacial charge transfer and the separation of photoinduced carriers [49]. The above XPS results indicate strong interfacial charge interactions for NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions.

The adsorption of pollutant molecules in the photocatalytic process is a prerequisite for the photocatalytic activity. The adsorption capacities of Bi2O4, Bi2O2CO3, NCQDs/BO-X and BO/BOC-X composites were evaluated by monitoring the light absorption changes of MO and BPA solutions. As can be seen from Fig. S3, after the solution containing contaminants and photocatalysts is stirred in dark for 1 h to reach adsorption equilibrium, Bi2O2CO3 exhibits extremely insignificant adsorption behavior for both MO and BPA. The adsorption capacity of NCQDs/BO-X and BO/BOC-(3-7) composites is higher than that of pure Bi2O4 for MO and BPA. When the content of Bi2O2CO3 in the composites is further increased, such as BO/BOC-9 and BO/BOC-11, their adsorption performance is slightly less than that of pure Bi2O4. Furthermore, Bi2O4 and all composites show a higher adsorption performance toward MO than BPA.

The catalytic activities of Bi2O4, Bi2O2CO3, NCQDs/BO-X and BO/BOC-X heterojunctions were evaluated by the decomposition of MO and BPA under simulated sunlight. The degradation efficiency of MO by different catalysts is shown in Fig. 4 a. Apparently, pristine Bi2O2CO3 shows negligible catalytic performance toward the MO removal, which may be related to its poor adsorption performance. The degradation rate of pure Bi2O4 is only 51% within 40 min. Controlling the precursors’ feeding ratio X in the range of 1.5∼11 during the hydrothermal process, all as-prepared NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions exhibit better photocatalytic activities than Bi2O4. For NCQDs/BO-1.5 and NCQDs/BO-2, their degradation efficiencies toward MO reach to 65% and 67%, respectively, within the same time period. As for a series of BO/BOC-X junctions, their photocatalytic activities are firstly enhanced with the increase of X. When X is up to 7, the BO/BOC-7 has the highest photocatalytic activity. After the catalytic reaction for 40 min, its degradation rate for MO is as high as 87%. Further raising the X to 9∼11, the photocatalytic activities of junctions start to drop, although their behaviors are still superior to Bi2O4, which may be due to the excessive Bi2O2CO3 that hinders the light absorption. Interestingly, the catalytic performance of the optimal BO/BOC-7 is much higher than that of NCQDs/Bi2O4. This means that the fabrication of the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junction is more effective in promoting the catalytic activity of Bi2O4 than the formation of NCQDs/Bi2O4 heterostructure under simulated solar light, using the same raw materials, NCQDs and NaBiO3·2H2O. The kinetic behavior can be described as ln (C/C1) = kt [50]. The corresponding reaction rate constant k for different catalysts is exhibited in Fig. 4b. It can be seen that all NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions are more efficient than pure Bi2O4 and pure Bi2O2CO3 for the degradation of MO. Among them, the k of NCQDs/BO-2 is 0.02867min−1, about 1.6 times and 47.8 times as high as that of pristine Bi2O4 (0.0179min−1) and pristine Bi2O2CO3 (0.0006min−1), respectively. Similarly, in accordance with the photocatalytic activity, BO/BOC-7 has the highest k of 0.05248 min−1 among all heterojunctions, which is 2.93 times and 87.4 times higher than that of pristine Bi2O4 and pristine Bi2O2CO3.

In addition, another colorless chemical pollutant such as BPA was also used to measure the catalytic performance of catalysts. As shown in Fig. 4c, after 125 min of simulated solar light exposure, the degradation rates of BPA by pure Bi2O2CO3 and pure Bi2O4 are about 23% and 46%, respectively. Obviously, almost all of NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions have better BPA degradation efficiency than single-phase catalysts, similar to the results of MO degradation. The degradation efficiencies of BPA by NCQDs/BO-1.5 and NCQDs/BO-2 are 57% and 75%, respectively. It should be noted that among all junctions, including NCQDs/BO and BO/BOC, two types of heterostructures, the BO/BOC-5 sample has a removal rate of 85% for BPA, showing the best photocatalytic activity. Once again, the photocatalytic activities results of BPA reveal that Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction is a better choice to improve the catalytic activity of Bi2O4 than NCQDs/Bi2O4 heterostructure. The reaction rate constant k is calculated according to the kinetic equation d[C]/dt=-K[C] [50]. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, the k of NCQDs/BO-1.5 is 0.00566min−1, which is about 1.33 times and 2.78 times as high as that of pure Bi2O4 (0.00426min−1) and pure Bi2O2CO3(0.00203min−1), respectively, while the k value of NCQDs/BO-2 is 0.01011min−1, which is 2.37 times and 4.98 times higher than that of pristine Bi2O4 and pristine Bi2O2CO3. Similar to the catalytic activities results, BO/BOC-5 presents the highest rate constant toward the removal of BPA, up to 0.01312min−1, among all NCQDs/BO and BO/BOC heterojunctions, which is 3.07 times and 6.46 times than that of pure Bi2O4 and pure Bi2O2CO3, respectively.

As a significant parameter for the paractical appilitaion, the photostability of photocatalyst was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, after five cycles’ running, the removal rates of MO and BPA are obviously decreased, indicating that the stability of NCQDs/BO and BO/BOC needs to be further improved.

In a word, both types of junctions show enhanced photocatalytic degradation activity for MO and BPA under simulated sunlight irradiation compared with Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3. For the NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid, the activity enhancement may be due to the excellent electronic conductivity of NCQDs, which can act as the electron mediator to accelerate the separation of photoinduced carriers of Bi2O4. For the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composite, the interfacial charges transfer and separation between plate-like Bi2O2CO3 and rod-like Bi2O4 should be responsible for the enhanced catalytic efficiency. Additionaly, the better adsorption behavior of two types of junctions, especially the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction toward the pollutants should also contribute to improve the photocatalytic performance to some extent. Notably, the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composite is more effective than the NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid in promoting the photocatalytic behavior of Bi2O4.

The optical absorption ability of Bi2O4, Bi2O2CO3, NCQDs/BO-X and BO/BOC-X heterojunctions was measured by the UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS). As described in Fig. 5 a, the absorption band edge of Bi2O4 is about 620 nm. The absorption cut-off wavelength of Bi2O2CO3 is located at 375 nm, and a strong tailing absorption peak in the visible region is observed for Bi2O2CO3, implying that as-prepared Bi2O2CO3 has a certain amount of oxygen vacancies [36]. After the formation of NCQDs/BO hybrids, a slightly blue-shift of the absorption band edge and a decreased absorption intensity are found compared with pure Bi2O4. For the BO/BOC-X junctions, similar optical absorption phenomena are detected. Furthermore, the higher the X value, the more prominent optical absorption blue-shift of BO/BOC-X heterostructures, owing to the bigger band gap of Bi2O2CO3 than Bi2O4. This result further confirms that the content of Bi2O2CO3 in the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composites is significantly dependent on the feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O during the hydrothermal reaction. Since both NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions exhibit the blue-shift phenomenon, the optical response behavior is not the reason for their enhanced photocatalytic activity when compared with Bi2O4. The Tauc plot:αhν = A(hν – Eg)n/2, can be used to estimate the band gap (Eg), where α, hν, and A represents the absorption coefficient, photon energy, and constant individually [51]. The n is determined by semiconductor intrinsic property, where n = 4 for indirect transitions [21]. According to relevant literature, Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4 are indirect band gap semiconductors. As shown in Fig. 5b, the Eg of Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4 are calculated to be 3.3 eV and 2.0 eV, respectively, consistent with the previous results [52,53].

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments were carried out to determine the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and specific surface areas of Bi2O4, Bi2O2CO3, NCQDs/BO-1.5, and BO/BOC-7. As displayed in Fig. 6 , no hysteresis loops are observed in the isotherms of Bi2O4, NCQDs/BO-1.5, and BO/BOC-7 samples, implying the non-porous structure of these samples [42]. The profile of Bi2O2CO3 exhibits a type IV isotherm with a H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of mesopores in the Bi2O2CO3 sample [33]. These mesopores should originate from the stacking of Bi2O2CO3 plates since Bi2O2CO3 plates themselves have no pores, as confirmed by the SEM results. The specific surface areas of Bi2O4, Bi2O2CO3, NCQDs/BO-1.5 and BO/BOC-7 are calculated to be 3.661, 6.319, 2.747, and 4.569 m2/g, respectively, suggesting that the specific surface areas are not increased after the formation of NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions. Thus, the specific surface areas are not responsible for the improved catalytic activity of NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrids and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species trapping and ESR characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Exploring the active species during the degradation reaction is of great importance to reveal its degradation mechanism. According to literature, Na2C2O4, IPA, and BQ are used as common scavengers for h+, •OH, and •O2 −, respectively [54–56]. As displayed in Fig. 7 a, after adding Na2C2O4 to the system, the catalytic activity of BO/BOC-7 is almost completely inhibited, indicating that h+ is the main active species for the photodegradation of MO. When Ar gas is introduced into the system to remove dissolved oxygen, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency is also markedly reduced. The reason is that e− lacks receptors in the absence of dissolved oxygen where superoxide radicals can't be generated, indicating that •O2 − also plays a certain role in the photocatalytic system. When IPA is added, however, the catalytic activity of BO/BOC-7 decreases slightly, showing that •OH plays a minor role in the photocatalytic reaction process. Based on the above results, h+ is the major active species, and •O2 − is the moderate important active species for the BO/BOC-7 catalyst. Similarly, as displayed in Fig. 7c, h+ and •O2 − are also the primary and secondary active species for the NCQDs/BO-1.5 sample. Simultaneously, the active species produced during the photocatalysis process were measured by ESR measurement. Under simulated sunlight illumination, the generation of •O2 − for BO/BOC-7 and NCQDs/BO-1.5 heterojunctions is detected in methanol with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrrorolin-N-oxide (DMPO) as the capturing agent. As shown in Fig. 7b, d, no signal is observed in dark. Nevertheless, the peaks of DMPO -•O2 − can be observed after 5 min of illumination, and the peak intensity increases with the extension of irradiation time. Thus, h+ is the main active species, while •O2 − is the secondary important active species for both BO/BOC-7 and NCQDs/BO-1.5 catalysts toward the photodegradation of MO.

The migration and separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers is a key factor affecting the catalyst's performance [57]. As displayed in Fig. 8 , the transient photocurrent response and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were recorded for Bi2O2CO3, Bi2O4, NCQDs/BO-1.5 and BO/BOC-7 heterojunction photocatalysts. Generally speaking, the stronger transient photocurrent densities, the higher separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers, and the better catalytic activities of samples. As shown in Fig. 8a, Bi2O2CO3 alone exhibits a weak transient photocurrent owing to its wide band gap energy. The photocurrent response of Bi2O4 is about 0.08 µA/cm2, whereas the photocurrent density of NCQDs/BO-1.5 is slightly increased to about 0.096 µA/cm2, which is about 1.2 times as high as that of Bi2O4. The enhanced photocurrent response indicates that the formation of NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid is beneficial for the migration and separation of photoinduced carriers of Bi2O4, given that the excellent electronic conductivity of NCQDs. Compared with Bi2O2CO3, Bi2O4, and NCQDs/BO-1.5, the BO/BOC-7 heterojunction displays a much higher photocurrent response of 0.2221 µA/cm2, which is about 2.8 and 2.3 times as high as that of Bi2O4 and NCQDs/BO-1.5, respectively. This manifests that the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junction not only promotes the transfer and separation of photoexcited carriers but also has a longer lifetime of carriers than the NCQDs/Bi2O4 heterostructure. This enhancement of photocurrent intensity should be assigned to the in-situ formed intimate interface contact between Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4, accelerating the interfacial separation of carriers, which can significantly improve the catalytic efficiency. As a matter of fact, the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composite does exhibit superior photocatalytic activity to single Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4 as well as NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid for the removal of MO and BPA under simulated solar light illumination. Moreover, the arc radius of NCQDs/BO-1.5 is much smaller than that of pure Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3, revealing that NCQDs/BO-1.5 has a much higher interfacial transfer efficiency of carriers because a smaller arc radius reflects a lower interfacial layer resistance and higher charge transfer efficiency (Fig. 8b). Compared with the above three samples, the BO/BOC-7 heterojunction shows the smallest arc radius, suggesting that it has the fastest carrier transfer efficiency. Obviously, the EIS results agree well with the photocurrent results.

According to the above characterizations, the charge transfer and photocatalytic degradation mechanism of NCQDs/B2O4 and B2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions are displayed in Fig. 9 . Firstly, the enhanced adsorption capacity of NCQDs/B2O4 and B2O4/Bi2O2CO3 composites, especially the latter, can enable a higher concentration of pollutant molecules in the vicinity of the catalysts, promoting effective contact between the catalysts and the pollutant molecules. The concentrated environment of contaminant molecules on the catalysts surface can accelerate the reaction with photogenerated active species, thus facilitating the photocatalytic degradation process. Then, as described in Fig. 9a, under simulated sunlight irradiation, electrons are excited to the conduction band (CB) of B2O4, and then equivalent holes are left behind in its valance band (VB). On account of the remarkable electronic conductivity of NCQDs, photoexcited electrons in the CB of Bi2O4 tend to migrate to NCQDs, and then these electrons are captured by the dissolved O2 to form •O2 −, which can oxidize the pollutants to CO2 and H2O. Meanwhile, the h+ left in the VB of Bi2O4 can directly oxidize the pollutants to H2O and CO2 due to their insufficient potential to oxidize OH−/H2O to •OH radicals. The enhanced charges’ separation efficiency, which is confirmed by the XPS, photocurrent, and EIS results, is responsible for the improved catalytic efficiency of NCQDs/B2O4. The possible degradation mechanism of B2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction is proposed in Fig. 9b. According to the DRS results, it can be calculated that the Eg of Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3 is about 2.0 eV and 3.3 eV, respectively. The empirical equation: EVB = X - Ee + 0.5Eg, ECB = EVB –Eg, can be used to calculate the CB and VB of semiconductor (X: the geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of atoms in semiconductor, Ee: the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale, 4.50 eV). The X values of Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3 are about 5.13 eV and 6.33 eV [45,58]. Consequently, the EVB and ECB of Bi2O4 are 1.63 V and -0.37 V, and the corresponding values of Bi2O2CO3 are 3.48 V and 0.18 V, respectively. Type II heterojunction will be formed between the contact interfaces of Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O4 due to their staggered energy levels (Fig. 9b). Electrons in the VB of Bi2O4 and Bi2O2CO3 are excited to their CB, leaving holes in their VB under simulated sunlight illumination. Partial photoinduced electrons of Bi2O4 will be captured by the dissolved oxygen to generate •O2 − radicals thanks to their negative enough potential. Another part of photogenerated electrons is inclined to migrate from Bi2O4 to Bi2O2CO3, while holes move in the opposite direction because both the VB and CB of Bi2O2CO3 are lower than those of Bi2O4. The holes transferred to the VB of Bi2O4 could directly oxidize and decompose organic pollutants because their potential is not positive enough to oxidize OH−/H2O to •OH radicals. This spatial separation of charges could undoubtedly improve the photoinduced carriers’ separation efficiency of the B2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junction, which could enhance significantly the photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, the photogenerated carriers’ separation efficiency of B2O4/Bi2O2CO3 is much higher than that of NCQDs/B2O4, which is confirmed by the transient photocurrent response and EIS results. This should be the main reason that B2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction possesses superior photocatalytic activity to the NCQDs/B2O4 heterostructure for the removal of MO and BPA under simulated solar light illumination.

In summary, a series of alterable products from NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrids to Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunctions to pure Bi2O2CO3 were successfully realized by adjusting the precursors’ feeding ratio of NCQDs/NaBiO3·2H2O (X) during the hydrothermal process. When X≤2, NCQDs, as an active component, are combined with the in-situ formed Bi2O4, producing the NCQDs/Bi2O4 composite. When 2<X≤11, NCQDs, as the carbon source, react with partial NaBiO3·2H2O to generate Bi2O2CO3, and then the residual NaBiO3·2H2O is transformed to Bi2O4, generating the Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterostructure. When X=12, NaBiO3·2H2O is completely converted to pure Bi2O2CO3 due to the sufficient NCQDs carbon source. Both NCQDs/Bi2O4 and Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 junctions exhibit improved photocatalytic degradation activity toward MO and BPA under simulated solar light, compared with Bi2O4 alone. Nevertheless, the latter is more effective in promoting the catalytic activity of Bi2O4 than the former. The reaction rate constant k of the optimal NCQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid is only 1.6 and 2.37 times higher than that of pristine Bi2O4, while the k of the optimal Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction is about 2.93 and 3.07 times as high as that of pristine Bi2O4, for the degradation of MO and BPA, respectively. The much higher photoinduced charges’ separation efficiency is responsible for the better catalytic performance of Bi2O4/Bi2O2CO3 than NCQDs/Bi2O4. This work supplies a convenient tactic to modulate the composition of hydrothermal reaction products and photocatalytic properties through changing the precursors’ feeding ratio.
